data.title: What Raising The Trade Drawbridge Means For The World Economy
data.description:
By Eric Liu | Eric Winograd

Trade wars threaten longstanding trade partnerships and could weigh on the global economy.

The early days of the Trump administration have brought sweeping tariff announcements. While the situation is fluid, the direction is clear: trade restrictions are likely to increase, with China as a primary target. Although there is still significant uncertainty, we think there’s enough information to soften the global outlook significantly.

Trade Barriers Dampen Growth

Free trade boosts growth; restricting trade slows it. We need only look back to the 2018 trade war for evidence: the global economy downshifted in 2018 and 2019 in the wake of increased trade restrictions.

The near-term impact on growth comes via multiple channels:

  • Tariffs raise the price of imported goods, reducing consumer purchasing power. Even before tariffs took effect, US consumer expectations of short-term inflation rose, and consumer expectations of the economy deteriorated.
  • Trade policy uncertainty affects business decisions. In the absence of clear policy, firms are reluctant to invest in future production. How can a business decide on investments without knowing how trade restrictions might impact a given project?
  • Financial markets have to wrestle with a less efficient growth environment — one in which growth may slow even as prices rise. Normally, when growth slows, markets expect central banks to intervene to support growth. But if price pressures prove persistent or inflation expectations rise, central banks may be less able to lend a helping hand in a deglobalizing world.

Trade Tensions Heighten Geopolitical Risks

Over the longer term, increased trade tensions raise even greater risks.

Ongoing trade tensions reflect the deglobalization trend we’ve discussed over the past several years. Deglobalization raises not only economic risks but also geopolitical ones. Trading partners have strong incentives to maintain stable diplomatic relationships to protect their mutual economic interests. Without shared interests in strong relations, global competition could become more pernicious.

Even if severe outcomes are avoided, countries may find themselves having to choose sides between the US and China, potentially accelerating deglobalization further.

To be clear, we believe we are far from the most severe outcomes. Our base case is that trade tensions remain more tension than conflict, and that restrictions slow but don’t stop the global economy. However, as tensions rise, there will likely be consequences, particularly for China.

China’s Trade Dynamics Are Shifting

The US and countries siding with it will likely raise trade barriers to Chinese goods to protect domestic jobs, to address trade deficits or for national security reasons. But unlike in 2018, China today is less reliant on the US and less vulnerable to economic disruptions from trade wars.

China holds an even higher share of global manufacturing than it did in 2018, accounting for 32% of the world’s total.* Since 2018, China’s exports to the US as a percentage of its total exports has fallen from nearly 20% to less than 15% (Display, left), and exports to the US now contribute only 3% to China’s GDP (Display, right).

China’s Dependence on US Trade Has Declined

And, while some Chinese goods are being rerouted and still end up in the US, the nature of China’s exports is changing. Products like mobile phones, electric vehicles and 5G equipment, which are not sold in the US, now make up a substantial share of China’s exports.

These shifts in China’s trade dynamics indicate a strategic adjustment to global trade tensions.

Picking Sides: A Double-Edged Sword

The critical question is what happens when trading partners are compelled to take sides, risking their bilateral trade agreements with the US. Recently, we have seen Mexico and South Korea indicate their intention to curb China’s backdoor entry for exports before entering trade negotiations with the US.

This complicates the identification of goods meant for re-export versus those intended for local demand. Countries might simply decide to deny all Chinese goods, which in our view would significantly impede Chinese growth. In this case, we would need to assess the impact of each country’s decision to reduce trade with China.

However, choosing sides in this way is a double-edged sword. Countries that have benefited from acting as intermediaries in the rerouting of Chinese goods would also suffer unless they can achieve full localization, including the transfer of intellectual property and increased local content. Asian countries that will need to make decisions include Vietnam, Malaysia, Singapore and South Korea.

Given its manufacturing capabilities, China’s economy is heavily reliant on exports. Yet, China currently accounts for only 12% of global consumption. Unless China can significantly increase its share of global consumption to absorb its manufacturing output, it will need to persuade trading partners to maintain their trade relationship.

This is particularly relevant for countries in the southern hemisphere, where China’s trade surplus has grown the most in recent years. Nearly 50% of China’s US$1 trillion trade surplus is with countries in the global south, with US$200 billion of that increase occurring in the past three years alone.

Not all this increased trade with the global south is due to rerouting. Chinese companies have been proactively exploring new markets and seeking new demand for their products to mitigate the impact of trade barriers and diversify their export destinations.

Another potential strategy companies might consider involves moving their entire supply chain, including intellectual property and operations, away from China. This strategy, similar to Japan’s strategy in the 1980s, would be especially important for companies focused on exports rather than on serving the domestic market or serving markets still receptive to Chinese goods.

Shaking Up the World Economy

Under deglobalization, trading relationships and even economic cycles are becoming increasingly disconnected and isolated. A less harmonized global regime, in which economic cycles vary more significantly across regions than they have in decades, suggests to us a less efficient world economy. Further, the trade-off between growth and inflation may become less favorable as trade wars progress. More inflation relative to growth would be unwelcome to central bankers and investors alike.

In our view, companies will need to carefully consider which markets to focus on, as well as decide on the technology, supply chains and materials they will use. Fractious trading relationships, brittle global supply chains, volatile inflation and growth dynamics, and potentially divergent monetary policy paths will likely complicate corporate investment decisions. In this environment, we expect companies to find targeting a global audience challenging.

Seeking a New Equilibrium

While the direction seems clear, the speed and magnitude of the journey are uncertain. Encouraging signs of technological innovation may offset some damage caused by trade wars. Policymakers currently pursuing trade wars may eventually conclude that the damage is too severe to continue, leading to greater stability over time. And we should not underestimate the resilience of the private sector and its ability to find solutions to new problems.

Though the near-term outlook appears challenging, we urge investors to maintain perspective. Deglobalization and trade tensions are not good for the global economy, but they need not be catastrophic. After more than 20 years of deepening ties, it may be inevitable that the world moves in the other direction for a time.

While periods of friction may be tough for the world economy and uncomfortable for investors, we believe that a new equilibrium will eventually be found.

*According to the World Trade Organization and the Center for Strategic & International Studies.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations, and do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams and are subject to change over time.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: SPY: Potential Valuation And Technical Support Levels
data.description:
Let me start by saying I do not have a crystal ball. I cannot tell you where the S&P 500 Index will trade tomorrow or next week. I also do not know how President Trump will respond to China's retaliatory tariffs on his 34% reciprocal tariffs, announced on April 2nd, 2025.

However, I do have a track record of warning readers about market excesses and complacency, so let me discuss how I see the current situation and at what levels I would start to increase my allocation to US equities again with broad index funds like the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY).

In my opinion, the current stock rout is a direct result of the market's overvaluation in the past few quarters coupled with Trump's tariff announcements. While the SPY ETF may have reached short-term technical support at ~510 (38% retracement of 2022 bull market), longer-term valuation support may not come in until the low 400s.

If the U.S. economy falls into a recession, investors should be prepared for an even steeper fall to potentially sub-$300 on the SPY ETF. For now, I continue to rate the SPY ETF an avoid (or sell).

Markets Were Overvalued And Primed For A Drop

For months, I had been warning readers that stocks were overvalued and vulnerable to bad news. For example, as recently as February, the S&P 500 Index (SPX, on which the SPY ETF Trust is based) was valued at a forward P/E of 21.7x (Figure 1).

This was the highest valuation level in the S&P 500 Index outside of the dot-com bubble in the late 1990s and the post-COVID stock bubble. As I wrote in my recent articles, "while high valuations can persist, high valuations can also lead to dramatic sell-offs" if markets experience negative shocks.

Liberation Day Was The Trigger

Apparently, President Trump's aggressive 'reciprocal' tariffs was the trigger that finally pricked the stock bubble (Figure 2).

From China at 34% to an uninhabited island full of penguins being tariffed at 10%, almost every country in the world got slapped with 'reciprocal tariffs' by President Trump.

Trump's tariff announcements were far harsher than market pundits had been expecting, bringing America's effective tariff rate to 20-25% (Figure 3). If the tariffs are implemented as proposed in the coming days, this will raise America's effective tariff rate to the highest level since the Great Depression in the 1930s, when the Smoot-Hawley tariffs took American effective tariffs to 20%.

Stocks did not like the tariff announcement, nor the retaliation measures from China, with the S&P 500 Index falling a combined ~10% over 2 sessions. The question now is, barring a dramatic reversal of President Trump's position and/or countries offering the U.S. 'phenomenal deals', where are the downside valuation and technical support for stocks?

Bear Markets Average 35%

Historically, in bear markets (measured as declines greater than 20%), stocks, as measured by the S&P 500 Index, fall by approximately 35% from peak to trough (Figure 4). Bear markets that coincide with recessions tend to suffer larger declines, and those not coinciding with recessions suffer smaller declines.

So, one question investors should ask is, what is the likelihood of a recession due to Trump's tariffs?

Is The US Headed For A Recession?

Unfortunately, if we look at real-time indicators like the Atlanta Fed's GDPNow indicator, the prospects for the U.S. economy are looking grim. From an expected ~2% real GDP growth rate in February, U.S. economic activity has plummeted in the last few weeks, with GDP estimates now squarely in the negative for the first quarter (Figure 5).

If economic activity continues to deteriorate, especially with the large overhang of Trump's tariffs and other countries' retaliatory measures, a technical recession is almost assured for the U.S. economy. This suggests the current decline in stocks has a ways to go yet.

We've Seen This Movie Before

In fact, this is not the first time Trump's trade war has tanked stocks. In 2018, during Trump's first term, Trump's initial trade war with China torched U.S. stocks for a 19.9% decline over 4 months (Figure 6).

The decline was only arrested in early 2019 as the Fed 'blinked' and shifted to an easier monetary policy.

Looking ahead, will the Federal Reserve come to the market's rescue once again in the coming days?

The Fed Is In A Tough Place

One factor hampering the Fed's ability to come to the rescue this time around is elevated inflation. Since early 2021, PCE Inflation, the Fed's preferred inflation gauge, has been persistently above the Fed's 2.0% goal (Figure 7).

In fact, recent data suggests core inflation may be inflecting higher, as businesses and consumers stockpile goods in anticipation of Trump's tariffs.

In a recent speech, Fed Chair Jerome Powell indicated that the Fed remains focused on inflation, as the U.S. economy faces "a highly uncertain outlook with elevated risks of both higher unemployment and higher inflation" due to Trump's tariffs being "larger than expected".

In other words, the Fed is caught between a rock and a hard place.

What Is The S&P's Valuation Support?

As I mentioned at the beginning of the article, the S&P 500 was reaching extreme valuation levels, trading at more than 21.7x FWD P/E. However, at what point would market valuations look more 'reasonable'? After the rout on April 3rd, the S&P 500 was trading at 20.5x FWD P/E (Figure 8).

If we adjust Figure 8 by another 6% (the S&P 500 Index declined by another 6% on April 4th), the S&P's valuation has fallen to 19.3x. Is this a buying opportunity?

In my opinion, we have not yet reached a buying opportunity based on market valuations. For example, looking at Figure 8, we note that the S&P 500 Index bottomed in 2022 at ~16x FWD P/E. This suggests there may be another 15% in downside before stocks become 'cheap' at 16x FWD P/E (or around 4350 on the S&P 500 Index and 435 on the SPY ETF).

Also, a 16x FWD P/E may be optimistic. Historically, the market's average FWD P/E is closer to 14x, with markets bottoming at ~10x FWD P/E in both the 1991 and 2009 recessions.

Do We Trust The E?

Furthermore, we need to consider what is the market's earnings. Currently, the market is expecting an 11% increase in the S&P 500's earnings for 2025 (Figure 9). However, in a recession scenario, earnings tend to decline.

Let us be optimistic and assume earnings do not decline, but merely moderate to 5% growth in 2025 (better than the 2022 experience), as companies are able to pass through the tariffs to consumers and demand remains robust.

Based on 2024 operating earnings of ~$245/share (Figure 10) for the S&P 500 Index, this suggests 2025 operating earnings of $257. At 16x FWD P/E, I believe valuation support for the S&P 500 Index is at ~4,100, or another ~20% downside from current levels.

Of course, if a recession occurs and earnings decline, the 'valuation support' levels will be commensurately lower.

Where Are The Technical Support Levels?

Moving on to technical analysis, one tool investors often use is Fibonacci retracement levels. Using 2022 lows as the start of the most recent bull market, we can see that the market has now reached the 38% retracement level around 5100 (Figure 11).

This also coincides with the market lows in August 2024 and a consolidation zone in March 2024, so I expect to see some near-term technical support. However, if this technical support fails, we may see a retest of the 50% retracement level near 4800 and the 61.8% retracement level near 4500.

Stepping back, we could also be completing the long term bull market since 2009. If this is the case, a 38% retracement of all the gains since 2009 suggests support at 4000, which is also where the markets rallied from in 2022 (Figure 12).

Deeper bear markets could target 3400 (-45% from peak or 340 on SPY) and 2800 (-54.5% from peak or 280 on SPY), although those levels of drawdowns are hard to imagine without the economy falling into a confirmed recession with earnings plummeting.

Risks of Being Too Bearish

Of course, the risk to my bearish analysis above is that the current administration's policies are often fickle, so tariff announcements could all be reversed next week if countries like China offer Trump a 'phenomenal deal'. For example, Trump dangled tariff relief for China if it agrees to his TikTok deal.

So investors should stay flexible, and be on the lookout for potential rallies if President Trump relents on his tariffs.

Long-Term Damage Incalculable

However, the long-term damage from Trump's erratic policies cannot be underestimated. Already, his threats to Canada's sovereignty (51st State jokes) have alienated Canadians and have led to boycotts of American products and travel. His threats to Greenland and treatment of Ukraine have likewise angered Europeans. American boycotts appear to be spreading globally, with countries all across the world looking at ways to reduce their dependence on American goods and services.

As an investor, we must consider the scenario where the U.S. alienates the whole world and becomes isolated economically and politically. In this scenario, would U.S. Exceptionalism persist?

Conclusion

In my opinion, the current stock rout may be reaching short-term technical support near 5100 or ~510 on the SPY ETF, as this is the 38% retracement of the 2022 bull market. However, to reach valuation support, I believe the markets may have further to decline.

For example, a return to 16x FWD P/E on 5% earnings growth (i.e. no recession) suggests downside to 4100 on the S&P 500 or ~410 on SPY.

If the U.S. economy falls into a recession, the stock declines may be even steeper, with long-term technical support at 3400 and 2800 on the S&P 500 Index or 340 and 280 on SPY. I will adjust my outlook as the market rout develops in the coming weeks and months. However, for now, the SPY ETF is definitely in the penalty box, in my opinion.

data.title: What Moved Markets This Week
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

It was a historic week for Wall Street - but not in a good way.

President Donald Trump's sweeping reciprocal tariffs announcement on "Liberation Day" resulted in the biggest rout in U.S. stocks since the market crash of COVID-19 in 2020.

The imposition of the steepest American trade barriers in over a century has hammered sentiment across the globe. U.S. growth expectations have rapidly soured, and recession odds have spiked.

Traders were caught off guard by the extent of Trump's tariffs, and responded by moving out of equities and into safe havens such as the dollar and bonds.

The benchmark S&P 500 index (SP500) cratered more than -9% this week alone, and now sits deep in correction territory after having fallen -17.4% from its most recent record close. The blue-chip Dow (DJI) also slipped into correction, ending the week -14.9% from its most recent record close.

The tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) fared even worse, slumping into bear market territory with a -22.7% retreat from its most recent record close.

Investors were hoping that Federal Reserve chair Jerome Powell would calm nerves at an event on Friday. However, the central bank chief said that the economic impact of tariffs was likely to be larger than expected, adding that the Fed was in no hurry to act on interest rates and would wait and see how events play out.

There was a tiny bright spot this week in the form of an unexpectedly strong March nonfarm payrolls report. However, most economists said that the jobs report was likely a last hurrah.

For the week, the S&P (SP500) retreated -9.1%, while the Nasdaq (COMP:IND) slid -10%. The blue-chip Dow (DJI) fell -7.9%. Read a preview of next week's major events in Seeking Alpha's Catalyst Watch.

Seeking Alpha's Calls Of The Week

Strong Buy Rating For Rivian Automotive (RIVN): Trump Tariffs Silver Lining.

Here's Why Verizon Communications (VZ) Has Become A Top-Tier Prospect.

BP (BP) Double Upgrade: Strategic Reset, Activist Push, Valuation Discount.

Seabridge (SA): Incredible Value As Momentum Builds On Record Gold Rally.

Moving To Sidelines On Ulta Beauty (ULTA): The Bad, The Ugly, And The Good.

VICI Properties (VICI): Downgrade On Relative Mispricing, Concentration Risk.

Turning Neutral On S&P 500 (SPY): That Was Much Worse Than I Expected.

Dropbox (DBX) Likely Entered The Phase Of Continuous Revenue Decline.

Deutsche Bank (DB): Great Time To Cash Out As Upside Appears Limited.

Wolfspeed's (WOLF) No-Good, Terrible Friday May Just Be The Beginning.

Weekly Movement

U.S. Indices
Dow -7.9% to 38,315. S&P 500 -9.1% to 5,074. Nasdaq -10% to 15,588. Russell 2000 -9.7% to 1,827. CBOE Volatility Index +109.3% to 45.31.

S&P 500 Sectors
Consumer Staples -2.2%. Utilities -4.5%. Financials -10.3%. Telecom -8.4%. Healthcare -6.5%. Industrials -9.4%. Information Technology -11.4%. Materials -8.3%. Energy -14.1%. Consumer Discretionary -8%. Real Estate -5.4%.

World Indices
London -7% to 8,055. France -8.1% to 7,275. Germany -8.1% to 20,642. Japan -9% to 33,781. China -0.3% to 3,342. Hong Kong -2.5% to 22,850. India -2.7% to 75,365.

Commodities and Bonds
Crude Oil WTI -10.6% to $61.99/bbl. Gold -2.5% to $3,035.4/oz. Natural Gas -5.6% to 3.837. Ten-Year Bond Yield -0.2 bps to 3.999.

Forex and Cryptos
EUR/USD +1.18%. USD/JPY -1.94%. GBP/USD -0.37%. Bitcoin +1.2%. Litecoin -1.2%. Ethereum -0.8%. XRP -0.1%.

Top S&P 500 Gainers
Lamb Weston (LW) +9%. Dollar General (DG) +8%. Molina Healthcare (MOH) +7%. Ross Stores (ROST) +4%. The TJX Companies (TJX) +3%.

Top S&P 500 Losers
APA (APA) -27%. Micron Technology (MU) -27%. Microchip Technology (MCHP) -26%. Western Digital (WDC) -25%. GE HealthCare Technologies (GEHC) -24%.

Where will the markets be headed next week? Current trends and ideas? Add your thoughts to the comments section.

data.title: Buying A Dollar For 60 Cents - 3 Dividend Bargains You Can't Ignore
data.description:
Introduction

"Buckle Up Buttercup" is what I wrote on December 20, when I issued a warning to (dividend) investors, as dramatic as that may sound.

Back then, my main thesis was sticky inflation, which was a big part of my "higher for longer" thesis. So far, that thesis has been right, as both inflation and interest rates have been higher than expected.

Unfortunately, the thesis has gotten an entirely new layer, as tariffs have become a much bigger deal than the market initially anticipated.

Using data from Fitch Ratings, we see that new tariffs would indicate the highest effective tariff rate since 1910, which was before the First World War.

Back then, the global superpower was the United Kingdom, which was called "The empire on which the sun never set," because it owned territories around the globe.

While I'm not going to get into the politics behind the tariffs, there is one major question: "Now what?"

  • Who is going to retaliate?
  • Will this accelerate economic re-shoring?
  • How bad will the impact be on inflation?
  • How big will the pressure be on the Republican Party in light of the midterm elections?
  • What will the impact be on deglobalization?

In the 12 hours after the tariff announcement, the dollar lost nearly 2% versus key currencies like the euro. This is exactly the opposite of what analysts expected before the election, as the market now sees a higher risk of the U.S. becoming isolated.

For example, if you allow me to paint with a very broad brush, major European nations do not necessarily need imports from the U.S. If we were to enter a massive trade war without concessions (I do NOT expect that to happen), the U.S. could do longer-term damage to demand for its goods.

It also needs to be seen how much pressure China can take.

China is hit hard, as additional tariffs push total tariffs to nearly 70%. Meanwhile, the U.S. is also taxing Vietnam, Thailand, and Indonesia at more than 30%. These are the nations where China has moved a lot of production to avoid initial tariffs.

This is what my friend and geopolitical expert Tony Nash wrote on X (formerly known as Twitter) after the tariff announcement:

China can subsidize 10-20% tariffs for a few months. They CANNOT subsidize a 54% tariffs. This will have COLOSSAL impacts on China. They can't fix domestic real estate, subsidize industries, build their military, fund the Belt & Road, etc, etc with this level of tariffs from their largest customer. - Tony Nash (via X)

All things considered, one thing is for sure which is that there are multiple geopolitical and geoeconomic strategies at play here, none of which are obvious to anyone without access to meetings in the White House.

That's why markets are so nervous - to put it mildly.

Personally, I cannot enjoy the carnage my portfolio is experiencing. However, I am very happy that I still have a lot of cash, relatively speaking. Despite one new investment and some additions, I still have close to 10% cash.

What better time to deploy cash than when markets are in turmoil?

Hence, in the second part of this article, I decided to focus on three dividend (growth) stocks that each come with a fantastic business model to potentially beat the market going forward.

However, to justify the title of this article, they also all have a valuation that is realistically (no clickbait) more than 40% below their current price.

So, let's start with a victim of U.S. tariffs.

Canadian National Railway (CNI) - Buying Assets Up North When Nobody Else Will

I love doing the opposite of what the masses are doing, as cheesy as that may sound.

Generally speaking, that's not always the best idea, though. For example, being consistently bearish during bull markets is a great way to apply this strategy all the way to the poorhouse.

I'm applying it differently.

Whenever the market starts to dislike high-quality stocks for reasons that I consider temporary, I start to get excited.

To me, it's a low-risk way of getting top-tier companies at great prices.

One area I like is Canadian railroads. Personally, I own Canadian Pacific Kansas City (CP), which is the only Class I railroad with direct access to all three North American nations.

However, it yields less than 1%, which makes it a less-than-optimal dividend stock for many investors.

That's why I like its bigger peer, Canadian National, a company I would definitely own if I did not have the goal to run a rather concentrated portfolio.

Currently yielding 2.5%, the railroad has a fantastic network that spans all of Canada, the U.S. Midwest, and the critical corridor between Chicago and the Gulf of America/Mexico.

I also like its focus on high-margin bulk and merchandise, including petroleum/chemicals, grains/fertilizer, metals, coal, and forest products. These goods have limited competition from trucks.

Its network includes seven major ports, 23 intermodal terminals, access to three coasts, and major tailwinds like the fact that 65% of all North American railroad traffic originates or ends on its network. Just like all of its peers, it has a wide-moat business model that is extremely hard to disrupt.

Meanwhile, its dividend has been hiked for 29 consecutive years, with a 5% hike for 2025, which is decent in light of macroeconomic and geopolitical headwinds. Since 1996, the dividend has grown by 15% per year.

After the pandemic, the railroad has also accelerated its buybacks, as investors wanted Canadian National to become more aggressive, as it has lagged behind its peers when it comes to buying back stock.

Given its A-rated balance sheet, it certainly has the ability to deploy most of its free cash flow through buybacks and dividends.

Additionally, despite tariff risks, the company stuck to its outlook of 10-15% EPS growth this year, supported by 11% higher volumes in January and a 7-8% rebound in March after weather-disrupted weakness in February.

According to the company's comments in March, the potential for escalating U.S.-Canada tariffs (i.e., on steel, aluminum, and electricity) remains a concern. However, CNI has not yet seen significant shifts in customer behavior, which is confirmed by its peer, Canadian Pacific.

Through at least 2026, CNI expects high single-digit annual EPS growth, outperforming economic growth with strong pricing power.

With regard to its valuation, analysts are a bit more upbeat than the company, expecting 11-13% annual EPS growth through at least 2027.

When applying its ten-year average P/E ratio of 21.3x, we get a fair price target of $147 in New York, almost 50% above the current price, as CNI trades at less than 20x earnings.

While cyclical and tariff risks apply, I believe CNI is one of the highest-quality stocks trading at a steep discount.

Carlisle Companies (CSL) - Much Cheaper Than 40%

I have mentioned Arizona-based Carlisle Companies a few times this year. Maybe even to the point where I'm annoying some people.

However, I believe it's too good not to mention, as I even brought the company up in a recent article titled "If I Could Buy Just 3 Dividend Stocks, These Would Be My Selections."

Carlisle has been one of the best compounders for many decades, as its management has consistently found a way to offer the right products at the right time.

Currently yielding 1.2%, Carlisle has a payout ratio of a mere 18% and a five-year CAGR of 15%. Its most recent hike, on August 1, was even above that, as the Board approved a 17.6% dividend hike. The dividend has been hiked for almost 50 consecutive years, as Carlisle is three years away from becoming a Dividend King.

The reason I expect this dividend to remain attractive is that Carlisle has made a major shift in recent years, becoming a 100% building materials-focused company with a focus on remodeling demand for commercial buildings.

This is a major market with a $70 billion volume that benefits from factors like economic re-shoring and the fact that the average commercial building in the U.S. is older than 55 years. Buildings around that age require major maintenance, which I consider one of the biggest secular investment opportunities on the market.

Carlisle is in a fantastic spot to service demand, as it has a stunning 25% return on invested capital, which is indicative of its ability to achieve extremely high returns when it invests in CapEx.

Moreover, despite being a cyclical company, it has a history of growing profitability (margins) even during tough times like the Global Financial Crisis and the 2020/2021 pandemic.

When adding strategic M&A to address demand in areas like energy-efficient insulation, we get a company with a major footprint and proven track record in an industry I expect to outperform the industrial sector for many years to come.

So, why is Carlisle so much undervalued if it's this great?

That's because of current cyclical headwinds and political uncertainty, as the company explained during its 4Q24 earnings call in February:

Carlisle continued to experience broad market headwinds more heavily weighted to the residential new and R&R markets and the commercial new construction markets. These headwinds included higher interest rates, restricting lending conditions and unfavorable weather patterns. These factors negatively impacted sales, and the results were below our midyear 2024 outlook. - CSL 4Q24 Earnings Call (emphasis added)

The good news is that the company is sticking to its Vision 2030 plan, which sees at least $40 in EPS, mid-teen annual EPS growth, north of 5% annual organic revenue growth, and an impressive EBITDA margin of 25%.

With regard to its valuation, the stock has a ten-year P/E ratio of 17.8x. Applying its Vision 2030 EPS target, we get a fair stock price of $712, more than twice as high as its current stock price.

A more short-term approach is also possible, as analysts expect 11%, 12%, and 13% EPS growth in 2025, 2026, and 2027, respectively. This paves the way for a more moderate and shorter-term price target of $500, 46% above the current price.

With that said, I was not expecting to be able to put the next company in this article, at least not before the tariff news hit the market.

Prologis (PLD) - We Still Need Warehouses

Tariffs are expected to hurt global trade.

Generally speaking, the higher the tariffs, the more pressure on globalization, as it becomes relatively more expensive to ship goods across borders.

That's bad for a wide range of companies, including owners of major warehouses, which are critical at almost every stage of every supply chain you can think of.

That's where Prologis comes in, the producer of the overview above and the world's leading warehouse owner, with close to 5,900 buildings covering roughly 1.3 billion square feet on four continents, with 86% of its net operating income coming from the United States.

While I cannot deny that tariffs cause warehousing demand risks, I love Prologis for a wide range of reasons, including the fact that it caters to some of the world's leading consumer-focused companies and transportation giants. These companies rely on a dense and efficient network of warehouses that many landlords cannot provide.

Just a third of its customers are cyclical companies that have witnessed demand weakness since the inflation surge of 2022. The overwhelming majority of goods flowing through its warehouses are daily basic needs and/or e-commerce goods that enjoy strong secular growth.

Moreover, and this is super important, unlike warehouses tied to manufacturing, its properties serve major urban centers where demand remains steady regardless of where goods are produced.

Especially in light of tariffs, that's a winning strategy.

It also has one of the best balance sheets among all public REITs, as its average weighted rate on debt is just 3.2%. It has a weighted average remaining term of 9.0 years, and a credit rating of A (or equivalent) from Moody's and S&P Global.

On top of that, while it needs to be seen how disruptive tariffs are on the global economy, in general, the company sees recovering fundamentals, supported by more transactions, bottoming prices, and a steep decline in new construction.

While I am writing this, Prologis shares are trading close to the lower bound of their four-year trading range, which usually provides investors with a buying opportunity.

This stock price decline has pushed its yield to 3.7%, which comes with a five-year CAGR of 12.4%. Its most recent hike was just 5%, which is due to ongoing challenges.

However, given the slowly improving fundamentals (despite tariffs), I believe dividend growth will pick up in the years ahead. It's also backed by analyst estimates, as consensus expectations see 9-11% average annual per-share adjusted funds from operations ("AFFO") growth through at least 2027 (FactSet).

Applying its long-term average AFFO multiple of 27.3x, we get a fair stock price target of roughly $150, 44% above the current price.

Although tariff fears will likely keep a lid on the stock market for a while, I believe all of these companies have what it takes to outperform the market, generate sustainable dividends, and deliver substantial long-term dividend growth.

Takeaway

My "higher for longer" inflation and rates call was right.

Unfortunately, tariffs have cranked up the pressure, pushing U.S. duties to their highest level since 1910.

Hence, as we have found out the hard way, markets have become extremely nervous, which makes sense, as trade wars are messy.

However, as always, chaos breeds bargains, which is why I am not losing sleep.

Instead, I like to focus on high-quality companies that can deliver returns we wouldn't find outside of corrections.

Here's a quick summary of my three picks:

  • Canadian National: A rail titan with pricing power, a 29-year dividend streak, and a network built to outlast any trade war. At 20x earnings, it's 50% undervalued.
  • Carlisle: A near-Dividend King, which dominates commercial building materials. Given secular tailwinds and its attractive valuations, I think it's a steal.
  • Prologis: This giant has irreplaceable warehousing assets and a 3.7% yield. At 44% below fair value, it's a bet on logistics surviving any trade storm.

Here are the risks:

Risks To My Thesis

Trade wars (we're finding this out the hard way), higher rates, or a deeper slowdown could pressure railroad volumes, construction activity, and warehouse demand.

Although all three stocks have fantastic business models, they are not immune to temporary capital losses. That's also why they are currently so attractively valued.

data.title: From Enbridge To Enterprise Products Partners: I Just Optimized My Dividend Aristocrat Portfolio
data.description:
How To Earn Buffett-Like Returns From High-Yield Aristocrat Bargains Hiding In Plain Sight

My Family's ZEUS LEGACY Family Charity fund is basically organized like this.

  • 25% high-yield, low volatility aristocrats.
  • 25% high-growth Ultra SWANs at reasonable to great prices.
  • 25% bonds (futures long or short)
  • 25% commodities (futures long or short)

In other words, market corrections tend to increase value, growth, and non-correlated assets.

For the high-yield low volatility Ultra SWAN bucket, the goal is the bluest of blue chips, those companies you can say with near certainty (95%-plus based on credit ratings) that these are stable businesses with dependable dividends.

8 Of My Favorite Low Volatility High-Yield Dividend Aristocrats (ZEUS Watchlist)

As you can see, my favorite low-volatility Ultra SWAN aristocrats are not that attractively priced right now.

Philip Morris (PM) is in a near bubble.

  • Approaching 50% premium trim/sell rating.

Philip Morris is in a bubble and 44% overvalued historically. If you buy it today, the fundamentals justify no return for three years.

In other words, this wonderful blue chip has priced in three years of industry-leading growth.

This is How You Earn Buffett-Like Returns, Hiding In Plain Sight

In the fall of 2020, PM was modestly undervalued and yielding 6.7% (7% at times).

Now it yields half that as Mr. Market has become irrational again.

What's my point? If you owned all of these aristocrats and bought them today, you can expect a 5.4% yield and 5.6% growth (FactSet consensus) or an 11% CAGR long term.

  • 12% CAGR over the next five years thanks to a modest discount.

But if you wait to buy each of these aristocrats at fair value, you can get a 6% yield with 6% growth or 12% growth.

If you buy each of them at undervaluation, you can achieve approximately 13% CAGR long-term returns.

  • Ultra value 35% discount becomes available at times.
  • Realty Income (O) today, BTI (50% undervalued back in 2023).

A 35% discount is 54% upside to fair value over 30 years, meaning reversion buys you 1.5% CAGR long-term valuation boost.

  • 12% yield + growth + 1.5% value boost = 13.5% CAGR.
  • The Nasdaq's 40-year historical return.

Low volatility high-yield aristocrats... or the Nasdaq, or both.

  • They tend to "work" at different times.

This is the key to my "Dividends and Dynamite" investing barbell strategy.

  • High-yield aristocrats for income today.
  • Hyper-growth capital gains to power future dividend growth through annual rebalancing.

But guess what happens if you sell overvalued aristocrats (like PM) to buy more attractively valued aristocrats?

ZEUS High-Yield Watchlist Ordered By Valuation

All eight offer 18% upside return potential in the next year. Excellent.

But if you buy the most undervalued aristocrats on this watchlist? Federal Realty has 76% upside potential and Realty Income (O) has 45%.

NNN (NNN) has 36% upside potential, and British American has a solid 23%.

In other words, look what happens if you only select the four best valued aristocrats.

The five-year CAGR consensus rises to 14%, but in the next year, the upside potential is 45% (like Realty Income), but for four companies instead of one.

  • Lower volatility and lower fundamental risk, too.

That's 27% better 12-month return potential.

Does that mean these four companies will actually deliver 45% returns in the next year?

Probably not. It would require all of them to return to historical fair value within 12 months, which is unlikely.

But even accounting for taxes (15% to 20% long-term cap gains) 14% CAGR long-term returns are possible by simply cycling between the overvalued and undervalued aristocrats.

In other words, if you bought Philip Morris in 2020? At a 7% yield and are up 24% CAGR over five years? Consider selling it and buying something like Federal Realty or Realty Income.

  • You bought a 7% yield. And can sell it at a 3.8% yield and flip it for a 4.5% yield for FRT or 5.6% for Realty Income.

In other words, instantly boost income, and instead of risking flat returns for PM, which is priced for three years' worth of growth, you can potentially experience around 60% returns over the next few years.

  • 70% total return potential over 3 years = 19.4% CAGR

The probability of a blue-chip returning to fair value within three years is a lot higher than one year.

What about five years?

  • 5% annual growth for 5 years = 28% + 60% upside potential (over five years = 10% CAGR value boost)

In other words, 10% value boost/dividends + 5% growth = 15% CAGR total returns.

  • 12.8% CAGR post-tax returns.

The Nasdaq's 13.5% CAGR historical returns? Adjusted for taxes, that's 11.5%.

What did we just see? How high-yield aristocrat investing (actively buying and selling) results in historically Nasdaq-beating post-tax returns.

Why am I bringing this up?

I Just Sold My Enbridge

I just sold my Enbridge (ENB) shares and used the proceeds to buy Enterprise Products Partners (NYSE:EPD).

Please note, this is not a recommendation for anyone to sell Enbridge.

  • 10% overvalued = hold.

So let me walk you through why I did this.

Reason 1: Personal Family Reasons: Short-Term Tariff Risks

I just finished my taxes, and in addition to family emergencies, I need to come up with some cash. We can cover the expenses, but this shows the importance of the emergency buffer that the ZEUS LEGACY fund provides.

  • A small fraction of the margin power that Interactive Brokers offers us.

That emergency reserve is currently $42,600 (not counting the family emergency fund I created with two previous big ZEUS Sales).

One of my jobs as the steward of the ZEUS LEGACY fund is to protect the emergency fund.

The trade war, specifically the potential for global tariffs, is not a big threat to ENB's business.

But the markets can be irrational, and if ENB falls to a 15% discount, that represents a $50K loss for my family.

  • Family members are starting to lose jobs and putting additional strain on the ZEUS emergency buffer.

Record Limit Order Buying

I swapped out ENB for EPD because it offers a better overall opportunity for ZEUS in the next five years.

What ZEUS Looks Like From The Perspective Of Recession Risk

Name Effective Weighting Volatility %
Long Bonds (via CTA) 45.16% 8.23%
Long Precious Metals (via CTA) 22.31% 14.88%
Nvidia Corp 11.99% 24.14%
Realty Income Corp 9.77% 8.03%
Amazon 9.68% 13.89%
Brookfield Asset Management Ltd 8.46% 9.42%
Short Industrial & Agricultural Commodities (via CTA) 7.49% 5.46%
Short Energy (via CTA) 7.20% 5.26%
British American Tobacco PLC ADR 6.60% 6.29%
Enterprise Products Partners LP 4.80% 4.39%
Sum 133.46% 100.00%
Recession Hedges 82.16% 33.83%
Low Volatility High-Yield Aristocrats 21.17% 18.71%
Hyper-Growth Stocks 30.13% 47.45%

(Source: Morningstar, Simplify)

About 52% of the portfolio is low-volatility aristocrats or recession hedges, and if recession risk continues rising, the recession hedges will automatically increase.

In other words, I don't have to worry about the recession hedges. They dynamically adjust as the bond and commodity markets deem optimal.

  • Bond/commodity markets are priced at a 52% risk of recession.

OK, so ENB is being sold because it's a personal necessity for my family.

  • Due to mounting financial pressures that EPD doesn't expose us to.

But a 95% optimality score is pretty high. So why else is EPD a better choice for us than ENB?

Reason 2: Better Credit Rating

EPD is the only A-rated midstream in history, and the bond market is pricing in a 3% risk of bankruptcy in the next 30 years (S&P estimates 2.5%).

Rating agencies consider 5X net debt/EBITDA to be a safe level for midstreams, and EPD is 3.5X on its way to 3.1X in the next year.

For context, rating agencies consider 3X safe for investment grade credit ratings.

EPD's new policy targets 3X, which is impeccable given the 15-year duration of new contracts.

In other words, this is a utility with the same leverage as a cyclical company.

This lets EPD borrow at the lowest average rate in midstream, 4.7%. For an average duration of 18 years with 98% fixed-rate debt.

ENB's Balance Sheet Is Almost As Good

ENB's BBB+ credit rating translates to S&P estimating a 5% risk of bankruptcy over the next 30 years. At the same time, the bond market forecast 3.3%, which is similar to the 3% they estimate for EPD.

  • The bond market treats ENB as an A-credit rating company.

ENB can borrow for 60 years at reasonable interest rates of about 8.3%.

That's the "smart money," bond investors, saying that ENB will be around and thriving in six decades.

How about EPD?

EPD can sell bonds with maturities over 50 years, and the bond market thinks it will be around through at least 2077.

That kind of multi-generational investing is critical to harvesting the tax-efficient yield of EPD.

Reason 3: Slightly Higher Yield + Tax Efficiency (K1 Tax Form)

ENB has a 15% Canadian tax withholding, which you get back through a tax credit.

  • Turbotax Full Service handled my taxes, and ENB's withholding was easily handled.

However, my tax preparer confirmed that EPD would not trigger any complexity for future tax filings.

There's a small risk that UBTI (unrelated taxable income) might at some point exceed the $1,000 annual limit in an IRA, requiring tax filing and tax payments.

However, EPD generates negative UBTI in most years, and if there's a positive year, the negative years act as carry-forward negative losses that offset it.

In other words, owning EPD in an IRA is usually fine, and ZEUS is a taxable account, making EPD an excellent choice for a multi-generational perpetual fund.

EPD's distributions are treated as a return of capital, meaning they reduce the cost basis; they're not taxed (unless I sell my EPD units).

Once the cost basis hits zero (about $160,000 worth of income with zero taxes), they're taxed as qualified dividends, meaning long-term cap gains rates.

  • 15% for most people.

If you die and your heirs inherit your EPD units, the "step up" provision kicks in and the cost basis adjusts to the value of your EPD holding on the day you died.

In other words, the $160K position I bought = $160K in tax-deferred income.

If I die and EPD units are worth $1 million, my heirs will then get that $160K tax-free income benefit made permanent.

They can then sell that $1 million worth of EPD tax-free (generating approximately $70K in annual income).

  • Because the cost basis rises to $1 million and there is no taxes on no capital gains.

I recommend that my heirs keep those EPD units because they can then collect $1 million in income tax-free.

And if my heirs then die and EPD is worth $10 million, the cycle repeats.

  • It's similar to the 1031 real estate exchange rule.
  • Where properties can be sold and rolled into a new property without paying capital gains taxes.

Reason 4: Slightly Better Valuation

EPD is about 6% undervalued, while ENB is about 10% overvalued.

That might not seem like a big deal, but take a look.

Reason 5: 36% Better 3-Year Return Potential

ENB has about 8% upside in the next three years due to several years of slow growth expected.

When an overvalued stock experiences a growth lull, this kind of disappointing returns can occur.

Now take a look at the opportunity EPD offers.

The difference between ENB and EPD in the next three years, assuming both grow as expected and return to historical fair value within three years, is 36%.

  • $58,000 higher return potential over the next three years.
  • If tariff fears hit ENB, then the difference could be $108,000.

In other words, while EPD and ENB offer similar returns over the long term (11.6% for EPD and 11.1% for ENB), the difference in opportunity cost is vast in the next few years.

Bottom Line: Enbridge Is Wonderful, And I Look Forward To Adding It Back To ZEUS When It Becomes Undervalued Again

Let me be clear, I am NOT saying "sell ENB".

I have limits to buy a lot of ENB should it return to fair value.

However, for the short term, my family's needs make it 95% optimal to swap ENB for EPD.

  1. No Canadian tariff exposure.
  2. Higher yield (and more tax efficient)
  3. Better credit rating (lower cost of capital)
  4. Slightly higher long-term return potential (0.5%)
  5. Much better (36%) medium-term return potential.

For these reasons, I made the optimal decision to go from one 6% yielding low-volatility dividend aristocrat to a slightly better high-yield low-volatility aristocrat.

data.title: Pure Storage: The Stock Is Down 24% Since Hyperscaler Win
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Pure Storage (PSTG) has shifted from a legacy, product-driven revenue model to a "Storage-as-a-Service" (STaaS) model. Its STaaS offerings are seeing strong demand amid the GenAI boom, as businesses seek cheap but reliable storage to support AI training workloads. The company delivered a solid FY2025, with resilient recurring revenue growth and improved margins.

PSTG is now pursuing more hyperscaler opportunities after securing a major win in December last year, which could accelerate its long-term growth. The announcement pushed the stock to a record high. However, I believe the rally was overdone. Since then, the stock has dropped 44% from the peak, and now 24% below the pre-announcement level. Therefore, I'm initiating a Buy rating on PSTG, as its 23.2x non-GAAP P/E FWD seems reasonable.

A New Roadmap for the Hyperscaler Business

As a high-growth STaaS company, PSTG is undoubtedly benefiting from the secular demand for a massive data storage. According to the recent earnings slide deck, the company has gained traction with Fortune 500 customers, and its energy-efficient storage solutions have received positive feedback.

In addition, PSTG has teamed up with leading AI and cloud providers. These companies include NVIDIA, Microsoft Azure, Amazon's AWS, and Google Cloud. On December 4th, 2024, the stock jumped 25% after the company announced a design win with a top four hyperscalers. I believe this is a game changer for PSTG as they enter the hyperscaler ecosystem. The magnitude of this rally signaled a massive growth potential for the company over the long term.

Demand for Subscription Services Remains Strong

The company's total revenue growth is largely driven by its Subscription Services segment. As shown in the chart, subscription revenue accounted for 46.4% of total revenue in FY2025, up from 34.9% in FY2023. This basically tells us that strong demand for data storage has surged since GenAI gained traction in early 2023. We can see that PSTG's subscription revenue growth has taken off, jumping 22% YoY in FY2025. However, its Product revenue segment has lagged over the past few years.

The strong growth in Subscription Services revenue is supported by its STaaS offering, Evergreen//One, which saw 20% YoY TCV growth in 4Q FY2025. Unlike traditional models that require large upfront hardware investments, Evergreen//One allows customers to pay based on usage, providing more flexibility.

The company has also maintained strong growth momentum in Annual Recurring Revenue [ARR], which is a key indicator of the sustainability of its future revenue. Although growth has normalized due to the law of large numbers, we can see the 21% growth in 4Q FY2025 remained consistent with its long-term trend. In addition, its subscription services net dollar retention [NDR] for FY2025 was 117%, still above the long-term target of 115%.

However, its total Remaining Performance Obligations [RPO] grew 14% YoY in 4Q FY2025, showing some softness compared to 31.4% YoY in 4Q FY2024, which disappointed some investors. RPO is a key growth metrics for software (subscription) companies as it reflects the total contract backlog that will be recognized as future revenue. The current slowdown in RPO growth could also impact the company's long-term growth outlook. During the 4Q FY2025 earnings call, management mentioned that the decline in TCV sales for their STaaS offerings in FY2025 contributed to the slowdown in RPO growth. I believe entering the hyperscaler business could be a game changer for PSTG, potentially driving a rebound of RPO growth in the future.

FY2026 Outlook Does Not Factor in Political Uncertainties

Despite better-than expected 4Q FY2025 earnings, beating both revenue and EPS consensus, PSTG dropped nearly 12% post earnings due to mixed forward guidance. The company's 1Q FY2026 revenue outlook fell short of consensus. PSTG expects FY2026 revenue to grow 11% YoY, which is slightly below the 12% growth in FY2025.

Non-GAAP operating margin guidance for FY2026 is 17%, down 70 bps from 17.7% in FY2025, but still above FY2023 and FY2024 levels. However, management are concerning about the macro environment this year, including geopolitical risks. Although they developed contingency plans to deal with potential tariff impacts, they signaled the risk has not factored into its FY2026 outlook yet.

How Trump's Tariff Policy Could Affect Pure Storage?

This week, Trump announced the Reciprocal Tariff policy, disrupting global supply chains, including semiconductors and storage hardware. Particularly, companies sourced chips and hardware from other countries like China.

If the trade war escalates, PSTG could face higher costs and lower demand. This could structurally affect the company's margins and growth outlook. The biggest downside risk to its FY2026 guidance remains macro dynamics, which are highly dependent on political factors. I believe the recent selloff has priced in some of these concerns.

Moreover, tariffs and export control are not idiosyncratic risks. Even the Trump administration has expressed support for AI-driven growth. So far, management’s base case assumes continued higher demand in FY2026.

Valuation Looks More Reasonable Now

The stock is down 35% YTD, largely due to the recent broad market selloff. It has now given up all the gains since early December and is trading 24% below where it was after the hyperscaler design win announcement. At this level, I think much of the previous frothy sentiment has likely been washed out.

PSTG is currently trading at 23.2x forward non-GAAP P/E, while its EV/sales fwd is at 3.5x, right in line with its five-year average. With strong demand for data storage and the potential for more partnerships with hyperscalers, there’s a chance for revenue growth to reaccelerate, making the current valuation look attractive.

Conclusion

In summary, PSTG remains a growth stock that continues to benefit from the ongoing AI trend. Its recent design wins with a top hyperscaler, shows it's working to build a strong position in the hyperscaler ecosystem, which could lead to growth reacceleration. However, we do observe the risks such as the slowdown in RPO growth and uncertainty around tariffs that could disrupt global supply chains. A potential recession will also reduce AI spendings in the short term. I think the 44% pullback from the January high has factored in some probability of the macro risks for FY2026. That said, the recent selloff indicates a buying opportunity as fundamentals still looking healthy.

data.title: BDC Weekly Review: CGBD Completes Its Merger
data.description:
Welcome to another installment of our BDC Market Weekly Review, where we discuss market activity in the Business Development Company [BDC] sector from both the bottom-up - highlighting individual news and events - as well as the top-down - providing an overview of the broader market.

We also try to add some historical context as well as relevant themes that look to be driving the market or that investors ought to be mindful of. This update covers the period through the last week of March.

Market Action

BDCs finished flat on the week with only modest volatility, in contrast to the preceding few weeks. Through March, only a couple of stocks in our coverage are up, however. Both MAIN and HTGC - the highest valuation BDCs have underperformed in the recent downdraft - a common pattern.

The average valuation in our coverage remains slightly above its historic average. It has come off around 7% from the recent peak.

Market Themes

Carlyle Secured Lending (CGBD) had its merger proposal with a private affiliated BDC CSL III approved. The transaction closed last week. Recall that CGBD got downgraded by BOA on the merger news. The view is that the merger will release previously private shareholders who will now be able to sell CGBD shares in the market, pushing the price lower.

CGBD is one of the few BDCs that is down on the year (-3.5% vs. a +2.5% average gain). The stock is slightly expensive still, but it’s worth watching in case it drops into attractive territory. Its 10.5% total NAV return over the past year is a bit below the median of 11.5% (though above the average) while its valuation is on par with the median (and below the average).

The valuation relative to the sector has come off around 8% from its peak.

Market Commentary

A reader asked about the view on the Palmer Square Capital BDC (PSBD). The discount looks attractive at around 13%. However, there are some issues as well. For one, leverage is one of the highest in the sector (perhaps second highest after Saratoga) at 1.5x vs. an average of closer to 1.1x. This is something that creates a large dose of downside risk. It also makes net investment income relatively inefficient.

Net investment income is only 42% of total investment income which is fairly low in the sector. This is also due to the very high management fee of 1.75% as well as the cost of debt which is slightly above average.

The company’s NAV has been steady recently, if on a slight downtrend, however there were earlier drops, particularly in 2022, which have not been clawed back. The company’s NAV was over $20 in 2021 and it is $16.50 now. The total NAV return over the past year looks to be around 6% - well below the sector average.

The key year looks to be 2022 when the stock lost about a quarter of its NAV.

If we break it down, we see that the majority of that was due to unrealized depreciation. However, what's more interesting is that only half of the unrealized depreciation was reversed over 2023.

More recently, the performance has been steadier if not on par with the overall sector. We would look for more stability in the portfolio before adding a position.

data.title: Otis Worldwide: Cautiously Optimistic Approaching Earnings (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

In my original analysis of Otis Worldwide (NYSE:OTIS), I assigned the stock a Hold rating, citing its premium valuation and weak momentum. At the time, I noted the strength of its business model—centered around stable, high-margin recurring revenue from elevator and escalator servicing—but questioned whether these attributes could justify its valuation given potential headwinds. Since that assessment, however, the stock has notably outperformed the broader market. This phenomenon gained my attention notably on April 3rd, 2025, the day of this writing, when the stock was modestly up by 25 bps while the S&P 500 sold off almost 5% on tariff jitters. While not much has changed about the underlying business performance outlook since my last article, it seems that the market is beginning to understand and appreciate the defensive qualities of this stock much more, giving me good reason to revisit an analysis. In this article, I will discuss the reasons why I believe a rating upgrade from Hold to Buy is now appropriate for OTIS.

Company Overview

OTIS is, of course, a global player in manufacturing, installing, and servicing elevators and escalators, with a leading market share of roughly 18%. The company's recession-resistant economics hinge primarily on its highly profitable service business, which accounts for around two-thirds of total revenue but approximately 85% of operating profit. Its recurring maintenance contracts are typically long-term and stable, driven by strict safety regulations and the essential nature of its products. Better yet, they are asset-light and produce very strong recession resistant cashflows. The company does the majority of its business overseas, which was highlighted as a risk in the prior article but will be explored with more depth today.

Since becoming a standalone entity after spinning off from United Technologies in the depths of the 2020 COVID crash, Otis has made significant efforts to bolster shareholder returns through dividends and share buybacks. Its dividend growth has been particularly impressive, doubling over the past four years, representing roughly 17% annual growth. While I do not expect dividend growth to remain so elevated over the long term, it has driven significant shareholder returns and brought attention to the stock from dividend-focused investors. Despite the underlying business having very defensive qualities, the stock has not traded defensively. However, over the last few months, the stock has appeared to diverge into a new era of performance which I will demonstrate in this writing.

Revisiting the Original Thesis

My previous analysis identified key risks, notably Otis's significant exposure to China and currency volatility due to its international revenue streams. Indeed, China accounts for a substantial portion of global new elevator demand and management noted in the most recent earnings report that 13% of 2024 sales were in CNY. Recent years have shown a steady decline in this market, with the company itself acknowledging multiple consecutive years of weakness. However, recent shifts in the currency market, especially the weakening of the U.S. dollar in response to new tariff policies, might create near-term tailwinds for Otis’s earnings given that a considerable percentage of its revenues come from Europe (23% of sales in Euros) and Asia. Management is guiding toward 4-7% EPS growth in FY 2025 but put that FX would be a headwind on earnings. With the DXY sliding this year so far and USD only representing about 30% of sales, earnings could receive a nice tailwind from FX over the next two earnings reports. It is unclear whether or not this dollar weakness will persist for a meaningful time or if it is just temporary, but one thing that was true about Trump 1.0 was that the dollar was weaker when Trump left office the first time vs. his inauguration, portending his willingness to wield a weaker dollar to accomplish trade policy aims.

Otis's share price performance relative to the broader market indicates a newfound defensive trading posture that it never seemed to demonstrate before. The company has now exceeded the returns of the S&P 500 by roughly 20% YTD. While this is not exactly surprising, it is unexpected since the stock did not really have a history of low correlation to the index. While it is unclear if this trend will continue, it is a positive sign for the company's prospects as a portfolio holding in the upcoming years of trade turbulence. Not only do the revenues of the company straddle multiple currencies and continents, but they are recession resistant and "essential", perfect for outperformance in a turbulent macroeconomic environment.

Valuation and Financial Performance

Since my last writing, nothing has materially changed regarding the outlook of the underlying business performance. It is still richly valued, especially when considering a mid single digit % EPS growth rate.

Currently, Otis trades at a PE multiple of approximately 25, reflecting continued investor confidence in the stability of its service-based cash flows, despite relatively modest revenue growth in recent quarters. Otis has maintained robust free cash flow generation, recently averaging around a 4% free cash flow yield. While not cheap, this valuation is beginning to appear more reasonable given the shifting macroeconomic landscape and relative stability of its earnings power. There are a few defensive industrial services businesses that come to mind, such as Republic Services (RSG) and Waste Management (WM) that have performed exceptionally well in recent months and are now much more richly valued than OTIS. Interestingly, OTIS and these stocks appear to be highly correlated with each other as of late, which is a bit of a departure from the way things were before.

One thing I highlighted last time was that shareholders were benefitting from overly aggressive capital return, whereby the company was increasing dividends at a higher rate than the growth of the underlying cash flows and that this implied there are not many avenues for value accretion through M&A. Interestingly, management guided that they would actually be buying back fewer shares in 2025 than they did in 2024, despite expecting roughly the same level of free cash flow, guiding to $800 million of share repurchases in 2025 vs. $1 billion in 2024. They estimate that this will accrete roughly 5 cents to adjusted EPS for the year.

All in all, the company recorded a fairly strong year for 2024 with adjusted EPS growing by 8.2%, and Service Portfolio units increasing by 4.2%. With Service as the primary profit driver and the company wielding very strong Operating Leverage, Service Portfolio unit growth in excess of inflation alone should translate to meaningful share price performance over time. Management did guide for Global installed base unit growth to be in the mid-single digits for 2025, implying another year of slow and steady compounding for Service Portfolio units. The market loves companies that are slow and steady with wide moat characteristics, and the trading behavior we saw as the broad market slid steeply is a clue that investors are warming up to OTIS as a defensive play.

Dividend Growth and Sustainability

It would be accurate to label OTIS historically as a dividend growth stock, as the dividend per share has basically doubled since the spin-off. However, I think it is more appropriate for prospective investors to think of the stock in the go-forward as a defensive industrial that happens to pay a growing dividend. While the historic 15-17% annual dividend growth rate should moderate to the high single digits over the long term, this remains attractive given current market conditions. Given its resilient cash flow and stable margins, Otis should comfortably sustain meaningful dividend increases, supported by ongoing share repurchases enhancing earnings per share. One thing I am very anxiously awaiting in this upcoming earnings season for OTIS is how much the dividend raise will be. I would prefer to see a raise exactly in line with their EPS guidance for the year on the high end of 7-8%, but we will see. While I would benefit personally from another mid-teens % raise, I would see it more positively if management were to increase the payout in line with the sustainable growth rate. That being said, I suppose management has some runway to increase dividends at a double-digit rate since the current payout ratio is modest at roughly 40%.

Technical Analysis

From a technical perspective, not much has changed, with the stock now approaching the all-time high once again. My previous article was written just after the price had failed to gain steam after briefly reaching a new all-time high and falling back somewhat. Now, with the macroeconomic backdrop looking modestly favorable for strength in the stock and earnings approaching, it is time to test a breakout again. With the USD weakening in the first quarter, there is a likelihood that OTIS could beat earnings on FX alone. While it isn't straightforward if this would lead to a breakout if it did happen, I am cautiously optimistic that we could be seeing a modest breakout in the near future. For potential buyers, watching closely for another strong volume breakout above recent highs or a confirmed bounce from lower trendline support could offer a compelling entry point, although I see the current level as being fine to enter as well. I would reassess if the stock once again failed to break out meaningfully.

Final Assessment: Upgrading to Buy on Greater Momentum

After re-examining my initial Hold rating on Otis, I now view the stock in a slightly more positive light due to shifting macroeconomic factors and recent trading behaviors. I specifically mentioned "weak momentum" as a defining characteristic in the title of my last piece, but namely I believe that this is either a non factor now or is soon to turn over. The company's strong service-driven economic model and resilient financial performance suggest its premium valuation might be increasingly justified in the current volatile market environment as investors come around to the defensive qualities of this strong, globally diversified business. I will be eagerly awaiting earnings to either confirm or reassess my near-term outlook on the stock, but my long-term outlook has remained bullish nonetheless.

data.title: Federal Reserve Watch: A 'New' Round
data.description:
Last month, the Federal Reserve announced a "new round" of quantitative tightening.

The current rounds of quantitative tightening began in the middle of March 2022.

The Federal Reserve began to reduce the size of its securities portfolio.

Here is the chart showing the "first round" going into the middle of September 2024.

The second round of "quantitative tightening" went from September 18, 2024 up until this past Wednesday, April 2. 2025.

The third round of "quantitative tightening," according to Federal Reserve announcements, is supposed to begin at the start of April.

And, here it is at the start of April, so the assumption is that the Federal Reserve is now in its third round of "quantitative tightening."

The Federal Reserve is still aiming to continually to reduce the size of its securities portfolio, but at a slower rate than it had been reducing it in the second round.

During the second round of quantitative tightening, the Federal Reserve reduced the size of its securities portfolio by $275.7 billion.

Furthermore, during this period of time, the Federal Reserve saw its "loans" to commercial banks decline by $94.7 billion, further reducing the dependence of the commercial banking system on the Federal Reserve.

The Federal Reserve has used these loan facilities to support the commercial banking system, as it helped the banking system adjust its reserve position throughout this period of monetary adjustment.

Note: there are only $4.0 billion of these loans still on the Fed's balance sheet.

So this second round of quantitative easing continued the Fed's efforts to reduce the amount of "cash" that the Fed had injected into the banking system to get the financial system through the disturbances of the early 2020s.

Note that the Federal Reserve System still has a portfolio of securities held outright of $6.4 trillion

How things have changed.

In the middle of March 2022, before the first round of quantitative tightening, the Federal Reserve held $8.5 trillion of securities held outright

Before the first round of 2020 year of quantitative easing, the Federal Reserve had $3.8 trillion of securities held outright.

And, during the Great Recession in February 2009, the Federal Reserve had $512.0 billion in securities held outright.

Can you believe that a little more than fifteen years ago, the Federal Reserve had a portfolio that was about $6.0 trillion smaller in terms of securities held outright!

My, things have changed!

The "excess reserves" in the banking system have also experienced a dramatic change during this time.

On January 9, 2008, there were $4.1 billion in "excess reserves" in the commercial banking system.

On April 2, 2025, the banking system held $3.4 trillion in "excess reserves"!

The banking system in the United States has lots and lots and lots of "excess reserves" around to lend out...to generate inflation.

And, this is where the banking system resides right now.

The Federal Reserve believes there is still too much "cash" on the balance sheets of the commercial banking system.

Federal Reserve officials still believe that the Fed needs a monetary policy that is defined as "quantitative tightening."

However, the Federal Reserve believes that it still must accommodate the political pressures that are alive and well in the world, but must not put too much further stress upon the banks by trying to reduce its securities portfolio too quickly going forward.

What about the Fed's policy rate of interest...the Federal Funds rate?

The Federal Reserve has been reducing its policy rate of interest as Fed officials feels comfortable reducing the rate.

Right now, Federal Reserve officials, to me, seem very cautious about taking the policy rate down further too fast.

Federal Reserve officials have done a very good job in getting the rate down to where it is now, but there is so much uncertainty being generated by the Trump administration with its tariffs and so forth, that these officials don't want to get into a situation where they move their policy rate down, but then must immediately return it to its previous level.

Federal Reserve officials don't want to get into a position where they are constantly forced to move the policy rate one way and then reverse themselves in the near future.

"Stability" is much sought after.

Chairman Jerome Powell has always been one to, if anything, err on the side of stability. This time around is no different.

So, the Trump tariffs are hitting the world and are being responded to.

World stock markets are reacting to all that is going on and will continue to do so.

And, uncertainty will seriously cloud the future.

Let us hope that Mr. Powell and the Federal Reserve are able to provide some source of stability as the United States goes forward.

data.title: Celestica: Valuation Is Too Attractive To Ignore
data.description:
Introduction

Despite Celestica's (CLS) stock is down 32% since my January 'Strong Buy' recommendation, I do not panic because this nosedive is explained by the overall stock market panic triggered by President Trump's tariffs. Meanwhile, Celestica's fundamentals demonstrate improvements. The latest quarterly performance was strong and there are several other positive developments as well. The valuation is very attractive after the sharp dip, and I am inclined to confirm my 'Strong Buy' rating.

Fundamental analysis

The company still enjoys strong AI tailwinds. Despite some slight deceleration due to increasing comparatives, the quarterly year-over-year revenue growth is still around 20%. Quarterly estimates from Wall Street analysts, suggest that Celestica will maintain double-digit revenue CAGR for at least the next eight quarters.

It is very important that it is not just revenue growth at all costs, but high-quality revenue growth. The company's TTM EPS is at record levels after more than tripling since the beginning of FY2023. And such an aggressive EPS growth is projected to continue, according to Wall Street analysts. Celestica's EPS is expected to more than double by FY2028.

Thus, the company is very efficient in creating value for its shareholders during the current AI and data center boom. Therefore, technology hyperscalers' plans to invest more than $300 billion in data centers during 2025 is a big industry tailwind for Celestica. To improve its positioning in the AI revolution, Celestica significantly ramps up innovation spending. The company's R&D budget has more than doubled since FY2022.

Such a firm commitment to innovation is not only crucial in driving future growth but is also important in building the company's image as an innovative employer. Everything looks good from this perspective as the company was recognized as one of Canada's top employers for young people for the second consecutive year. This is a crucial positive factor over the long haul as being one of the country's top employers means that Celestica is more likely to hire the brightest young employees with great potential to develop.

Celestica's robust balance sheet also positions the company well to invest in growth and innovation. The company is in a strong liquidity position with more than $400 million in cash. Celestica's total debt of around $930 million is mostly long-term, and it is around 10 times lower than the company's market cap. The company's tangible book value per share has demonstrated consistent growth over the last three years. In addition, S&P Global recently upgraded Celestica's credit rating to 'BB+'.

The company's fundamental strength attracts new investors, including institutional ones. For example, LPL Financial LLC boosted its stake in Celestica significantly over the last quarter. Overall, the number of Celestica's institutional investors has increased significantly over the last few years. Increasing confidence in Celestica's long-term prospects from institutional investors is a solid bullish factor, in my opinion.

Wall Street's analysts are also confident in Celestica's ability to successfully capitalize on AI tailwinds. The average target price is $140, which is more than twice as high as the last close. In addition, the stock also boasts a 'Strong Buy' rating from Seeking Alpha Quant, which is usually quite strong in stock picking.

Furthermore, the technical setup looks excellent. Celestica's 14-day RSI indicator is very low at just 31, meaning that the stock is oversold. Celestica's RSI indicator rarely dips that low, indicating potential for a solid near-term share price rebound.

Wrapping this up, Celestica's fundamentals position the company well to continue boosting value for shareholders by capitalizing on AI tailwinds. Increasing confidence from institutional investors, as well as massive upside potential, suggested from Wall Street analysts add significant optimism as well.

Valuation analysis

Celestica's forward P/E ratio trajectory looks extremely bullish. The estimated FY2025 P/E ratio is already quite low, below 15. Furthermore, due to the expected aggressive EPS growth, the FY2028 forward P/E ratio is expected to be as low as 7.4.

Now, the DCF it is. As there were no material changes in interest rates, I reiterate the same 8.3% discount rate. AI tailwinds remain quite strong, meaning that using a 3% constant growth rate to calculate terminal value ('TV') is quite conservative. Top-line assumptions for FY 2026-2027 were obtained from consensus because they represent the average opinions of at least 5 Wall Street analysts. For the years beyond FY2027, I incorporate a conservative 100 basis points yearly revenue growth deceleration. Celestica's TTM levered FCF margin is 3.25%. I am using it as FY2026 assumption and also applying a 50-basis point yearly expansion rate for the FCF margin. According to Seeking Alpha, there are 116 million shares outstanding.

The suggested fair share price is $116.5. This is significantly higher than the current share price with a 68% upside potential. I find Celestica's valuation extremely attractive.

Mitigating factors

It is highly uncertain regarding how long the broader stock market panic will take. Trump's administration has been quite aggressive with new tariffs so far, and it is possible that new big developments will emerge in this respect. Even the stock market's largest names like Apple and Nvidia experienced a massive stock sell-off this week. For smaller players like Celestica, share price movements are highly likely to be sharper. Therefore, I do not recommend buying CLS without a long-term mindset. I do not suggest to time the market.

It is also important to keep in mind that Celestica faces a massive customer concentration risk. According to the 10-K, Celestica's top 10 customers represented 73% of total revenue in FY2024. It is also important to understand that with massive revenue growth from data centers, the concentration risk increases because in FY2023 Celestica's top 10 customers generated 64% of the company's sales. High customer concentration is inherently risky, as it makes any company heavily dependent on its partners' decisions and financial strength.

Conclusion

My take is that Celestica remains a high-conviction 'Strong buy'. The company's fundamentals are consistently improving, which is recognized by institutional investors, credit agencies, and various reputable analysts.
